en.extract:
The reddish scops owl (Otus rufescens) is an owl found in southeast Asia, in the countries of Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia, and Brunei.


Description
The reddish scops owl measures 15-18 cm in length and has a body mass of 70-83 g. Body is generally a red-brown, with paler face and ear tufts. Displays black spotting across chest, and white spotting along back and wings; juveniles appear similar to adult individuals, but with less spotting.  
The call of the owl is a whistling “hooo," which is repeated in 7-11 second intervals. The calls of this species are able to be reliably sexed, with males having a higher pitched call than females. 


Taxonomy
The reddish scops owl was first described by Thomas Horsfield in 1821 under the binomial Strix rufescens. 
The owl has two subspecies: 

O. r. malayensis
O. r. rufescens


Habitat
The reddish scops owl lives in lowland regions including rainforests and swamps. The owl does not migrate, and primarily inhabits a home range of 1.31–2.16 hectares.


Conservation Status
The reddish scops owl is evaluated by the IUCN as near threatened, and its population is believed to be decreasing. The primary threat to these birds is habitat loss from logging; an estimated 14%-18% of the forests the owl inhabits have been lost in the last decade. This species is also threatened by capture and sale in illegal wildlife trade. 


Diet
While not extensively studied, the diet of the reddish scops owl consists of primarily arthropods and small vertebrates. 


Breeding
The reddish scops owl nests in tree cavities. On Java, eggs of the species have been found in March and April, with a nestling present by mid-July. 


References

BirdLife Species Factsheet
ITIS Report

The '''reddish scops owl''' ('''''Otus rufescens''''') is an [[owl]] found in [[southeast Asia]], in the countries of [[Thailand]], [[Malaysia]], [[Indonesia]], and [[Brunei]].<ref name="iucn status"/>

== Description ==
The reddish scops owl measures 15-18 cm in length and has a body mass of 70-83 g.<ref name=":2">König, C., F. Weick, and J.-H. Becking (2008). Owls of the World. 2nd edition. Christopher Helm, London, UK.</ref> Body is generally a red-brown, with paler face and ear tufts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Holt |first=Denver W. |last2=Berkley |first2=Regan |last3=Deppe |first3=Caroline |last4=Enríquez |first4=Paula L. |last5=Petersen |first5=Julie L. |last6=Rangel Salazar |first6=José Luis |last7=Segars |first7=Kelley P. |last8=Wood |first8=Kristin L. |last9=Marks |first9=Jeffrey S. |date=2020 |title=Reddish Scops-Owl (Otus rufescens), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/resowl1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.resowl1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Displays black spotting across chest, and white spotting along back and wings; juveniles appear similar to adult individuals, but with less spotting.<ref name=":1" />  

The [https://xeno-canto.org/27666 call] of the owl is a whistling “hooo," which is repeated in 7-11 second intervals.<ref name=":1" /> The calls of this species are able to be reliably sexed, with males having a higher pitched call than females.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mat Nawi |first=Mohd I. H. |last2=Sulaimi |first2=Ahmad S. |last3=Mah |first3=Amera N. M. M. A. |last4=Zakaira |first4=Mohamed |last5=Puan |first5=Chong L. |date=2021 |title=Pair duets and acoustic sexing of Reddish Scops Owl ''Otus rufescens'' |url=https://static1.squarespace.com/static/5c1a9e03f407b482a158da87/t/67e40998d292b7498d09bc13/1742997912675/e-JAO-37-Reddish-Scops-owl.pdf |journal=Journal of Asian Ornithology |volume=37 |pages=88-92}}</ref> 

== Taxonomy ==
The reddish scops owl was first described by [[Thomas Horsfield]] in 1821 under the binomial ''Strix rufescens''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Horsfield |first=Thomas |date=1821 |title=Systematic Arrangement and Description of Birds from the Island of Java |url=https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1095-8339.1821.tb00061.x |journal=Transactions of the Linnean Society of London |volume=13 |issue=1 |pages=140}}</ref> 

The owl has two subspecies:<ref name=":1" /> 

* ''O. r. malayensis'' 
* ''O. r. rufescens'' 

== Habitat ==
The reddish scops owl lives in lowland regions including [[Rainforest|rainforests]] and [[Swamp|swamps]].<ref name=":1" /> The owl does not migrate, and primarily inhabits a home range of 1.31–2.16 hectares.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Sulaimi |first=Ahmad S. |last2=Mat Nawi |first2=Mohd I. H. |last3=Mah |first3=Amera N. M. M. A. |last4=Top |first4=Marina M. |last5=Puan |first5=Chong L. |date=2025 |title=Home range of a Reddish Scops-Owl (''Otus rufescens'') in an isolated forest in southwestern Peninsular Malaysia |url=https://doi.org/10.1676/21-00016 |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=134 |issue=1}}</ref>

== Conservation Status ==
The reddish scops owl is evaluated by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] as [[Near-threatened species|near threatened]], and its population is believed to be decreasing.<ref name="iucn status"/> The primary threat to these birds is habitat loss from logging; an estimated 14%-18% of the forests the owl inhabits have been lost in the last decade.<ref name="iucn status"/> This species is also threatened by capture and sale in illegal [[wildlife trade]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Iqbal |first=Muhammad |date=2016 |title=Predators become prey! Can Indonesian raptors survive online bird trading? |url=https://d1wqtxts1xzle7.cloudfront.net/47626585/Online-raptors_2-libre.pdf?1469813773=&response-content-disposition=inline%3B+filename%3DIqbal_M_2016_Predators_become_prey_Can_I.pdf&Expires=1773178171&Signature=GgN4meG92ysNmNhHfYim9Ftj5MzwG5pPEdb2CfFxvy-6WNpoKxosaqugGWrl2jMObuaceq-85h7ZwABh6EFBpa1hapXwKVDjWOuocY1RyVs2Mu2nTFpEESEMl0DyfURbm21cXoxIIIzQvedrYlTh0CALkBAih7~vXYWKDLLOxrOSc8cyU7okPbe6YJ~S76ONFFvXG5qrSj7l22i5CbJWQbYpZW4RIm-xaseyqsU9pLDYCfEclsiAYkmkvvjxEgP8QPqb5S6w25ewmuQLWclTCcCp5Dfd0rs0DyCnFhNKZAA0AT9Sk~NeFyWL~2BHyZ~HSrkEycSeGsVm4iqTrXzzAw__&Key-Pair-Id=APKAJLOHF5GGSLRBV4ZA |journal=BirdingASIA |volume=25 |pages=30-35}}</ref> 

== Diet ==
While not extensively studied, the diet of the reddish scops owl consists of primarily [[Arthropod|arthropods]] and small vertebrates.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mohd-Taib |first=F. S. |last2=Rabiatul-Adawiyah |first2=S. |last3=Md-Nor |first3=S. |date=2014 |title=Birds Communities of Fragmented Forest within Highly Urbanized Landscape in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia |url=https://doi.org/10.1063/1.4895279 |journal=AIP Conference Proceedings |volume=1614 |pages=651-658}}</ref> 

== Breeding ==
The reddish scops owl nests in tree cavities.<ref name=":1" /> On Java, eggs of the species have been found in March and April, with a nestling present by mid-July.<ref name=":2" />
